beats.narration_text: I want to take a computer-use agent from a slick demo to something I trust in production. My worry is the gap between a scripted screenshot loop and a real workflow where the screen changes under the model. Help me stress-test that gap: (1) design a task where a modal or loading spinner appears mid-action so I can see how the loop handles unexpected screen state, (2) show me where to add explicit verification steps that re-screenshot and confirm before the next click, (3) suggest guardrails that stop the agent when it can't find the element it expects.

props.command: I want to take a computer-use agent from a slick demo to something I trust in production. My worry is the gap between a scripted screenshot loop and a real workflow where the screen changes under the model. Help me stress-test that gap: (1) design a task where a modal or loading spinner appears mid-action so I can see how the loop handles unexpected screen state, (2) show me where to add explicit verification steps that re-screenshot and confirm before the next click, (3) suggest guardrails that stop the agent when it can't find the element it expects.
output: The naive demo loop burns $50 fast on full-res screenshot tokens
Seven production fixes: resize, prune, batch, cache, compact, sandbox, record
Resizing screenshots to 1568px wide cuts token cost by 70%
Trajectory recording makes every autonomous action auditable and replayable

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The naive loop: take a screenshot, send the full image to Claude, get an action, repeat. The problem is that screenshots are expensive tokens. A full-resolution macOS screenshot passes twelve hundred tokens to the model. A ten-step task that loops ten times puts twelve thousand screenshot tokens through the context before any action tokens. This is the cost explosion.

artifactLines: Take a screenshot → send full-res image → consume all tokens → take action → repeat
A full-resolution macOS screenshot ≈ 1,200 tokens per step
A 10-step task = 12,000 screenshot tokens before any action tokens
The loop is naive and correct — but not production-safe
This cost explosion is the problem the seven fixes solve

beats.narration_text: The production version makes seven changes. Resize every screenshot to one thousand five hundred sixty-eight pixels wide before sending — this is the optimal size for Claude's vision. Drop screenshots older than the last few steps — the model does not need the history of the screen, only the current state. Batch tool calls where possible. Add cache-control headers to system prompts. Use server-side compaction. Run actions in a sandboxed shell. Record every action as a structured trajectory event.

props.artifactHeading: Seven changes between demo and deploy.
artifactLines: 1. Resize screenshots to 1568px wide (optimal for Claude's vision)
2. Drop screenshots older than the last N steps — model needs current state only
3. Batch tool calls where possible
4. Add cache-control headers to system prompts
5. Use server-side compaction · Run actions in a sandboxed shell · Record every action as a structured trajectory event

beats.narration_text: The screenshot budget matters. Before optimization: a twenty-step task might burn forty thousand tokens in screenshots alone at a cost that adds up fast. After resizing and pruning: the same task costs seventy to eighty percent less in screenshot tokens. The prompt caching puts another dent in it. The cost is not linear — each optimization compounds.

artifactLines: 1. One full-res macOS screenshot ≈ 1,200 tokens
2. 20-step task unoptimized → ~40,000 tokens in screenshots alone
3. Resize to 1568px → drops screenshot token cost by ~70%
4. Prune older screenshots → another ~60% reduction
5. Each optimization compounds — the savings are not additive, they multiply

beats.narration_text: Trajectory recording turns an autonomous agent into an auditable system. Every mouse click, every keypress, every screenshot is logged with a timestamp, the tool that was called, its arguments, and the result. You can replay any session exactly. When something goes wrong — and it will — you do not guess at what happened. You play back the tape.

props.artifactHeading: The audit log for autonomous action.
artifactLines: 1. Every action logged as a structured event: timestamp, tool, args, result
2. Any session can be replayed exactly
3. When something goes wrong — play back the tape, don't guess
4. Costs become attributable to specific actions and steps
5. Turns an autonomous agent into an auditable system

beats.narration_text: Here is the prompt. Paste it: Design a trajectory logging schema for a computer-use agent. Each event should be enough to replay or debug the session. Include: action type, target element, screenshot hash before and after, confidence score, and a flag for whether human confirmation was requested. Then ask: what else does the log need to make oversight meaningful rather than just nominal? The answer to that last question is what computer-use safety actually requires. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: Design a trajectory logging schema for a computer-use agent. Each event must be enough to replay or debug the session. Include: action type, target element, screenshot hash before and after, confidence score, and whether human confirmation was requested. What else does the log need to make oversight meaningful — not just nominal?
